strategy_summary: AquaLoop should treat hotels as a leading validation wedge for onsite greywater reuse, package the first offer as assessment-to-pilot, and block client-ready claims until property economics, engineering, regulatory, buyer, and naming evidence are validated.

slides.title: AquaLoop should start as a hotel water-reuse validation wedge
slides.key_message: Hotels are the best current hypothesis, but the product should be positioned as a validation pathway before it is sold as proven infrastructure.

content_blocks.heading: Recommendation
content_blocks.body: Lead with hospitality discovery: validate segment, architecture, code pathway, and property economics before making savings claims.

content_blocks.heading: What this means now
body: Hotels are a focused first wedge, not a proven market decision.
The first offer should create decision confidence.
Client-ready claims require measured proof.

slides.visual_direction: Executive title slide with a hotel building, water-loop path, and proof-gate markers.
slides.speaker_notes: Open by making the recommendation clear while emphasizing that this is an early discovery package.
production_notes: Final external brand naming requires legal review.
slides.needs_input_reason: The strategic recommendation depends on unvalidated buyer, engineering, and property-economic inputs.

slides.title: The strongest current asset is a disciplined proof path, not a finished product claim
slides.key_message: AquaLoop can credibly frame what must be proven, but it cannot yet claim savings, ROI, compliance, or installability.

content_blocks.heading: Safe now
body: Hotels are a plausible segment to investigate.
Onsite non-potable reuse is a recognized category.
Architecture, code, economics, and buyer ownership need validation.

content_blocks.heading: Blocked for client-ready export
body: Quantified savings or payback.
Regulatory approval.
Specific treatment performance.
Plug-and-play installation.

slides.visual_direction: Use a split evidence board with green safe claims and amber/red blocked claims.
slides.speaker_notes: Explain that this deck is useful because it protects the client from premature claims.
production_notes: Keep caveats visible, not buried in notes.
slides.needs_input_reason: This slide summarizes internal readiness rules and should be reviewed before external sharing.

slides.title: Hotels are a credible first segment to investigate
slides.purpose: Establish why the hotel wedge is worth discovery investment.
slides.key_message: Public hotel water-efficiency context supports investigating hospitality because hotels have multiple recurring water-use areas.

content_blocks.heading: Hotel water-use zones
content_blocks.body: Guest rooms, restrooms, laundry, kitchens, landscaping, and mechanical systems create multiple places to investigate non-potable reuse fit.

content_blocks.heading: Strategic implication
content_blocks.body: The segment is promising enough for focused validation, but public category context does not prove AquaLoop economics.

slides.visual_direction: Hotel section diagram with labeled water-use zones and a callout that economics are unvalidated.
slides.speaker_notes: Use this slide to justify the hotel hypothesis without treating it as proven.

slides.title: Water reuse lives inside standards and code pathways
slides.purpose: Explain why compliance and certification must be part of the product strategy.
slides.key_message: Onsite reuse is a real category, but the launch market and applicable pathway determine what AquaLoop can safely claim.

content_blocks.heading: Evidence signals
body: Onsite residential and commercial water reuse has formal standard categories.
Local examples show specific onsite reuse and greywater rules.
AquaLoop has no selected launch jurisdiction or certification path yet.

content_blocks.heading: Gate sequence
content_blocks.body: Launch market -> allowed sources and uses -> treatment standard -> plumbing review -> monitoring plan -> claim approval.

slides.visual_direction: Regulatory gate diagram with jurisdiction as the first gate.
slides.speaker_notes: Make clear that code pathway is not a footnote; it is part of the product.

slides.title: Competitors already occupy the generic greywater story
slides.purpose: Show why AquaLoop needs sharper positioning.
slides.key_message: Named vendors already use onsite reuse, greywater recycling, and shower recycling language, so AquaLoop should not rely on generic category claims.

content_blocks.heading: Implication
content_blocks.body: The deck should differentiate on hospitality-specific validation, not unsupported superiority.

slides.visual_direction: Use a plain competitor territory matrix with vendor-source caveats.
slides.speaker_notes: Use competitors as proof that the category exists and as warning against generic positioning.
production_notes: Add dated screenshot captures before any formal client presentation.

slides.title: The AquaLoop name itself needs validation
slides.purpose: Flag brand and legal risk early.
slides.key_message: AQUALOOP appears in the greywater reuse market, so the project name should stay internal until legal review.

content_blocks.heading: Naming risk
content_blocks.body: Use AquaLoop Hospitality as a working project label only; do not treat the name as cleared.

content_blocks.heading: Decision needed
body: Run legal/trademark review.
Decide whether to keep, modify, or replace the name.
Avoid external naming claims until reviewed.

slides.visual_direction: Brand risk card with a blocked readiness badge.
slides.speaker_notes: Treat this as a responsible early warning, not a legal conclusion.
production_notes: Legal review required before external export.
slides.needs_input_reason: Legal/trademark review is missing for AquaLoop naming.

slides.title: The first offer should be assessment-to-pilot, not system sale
slides.purpose: Recommend a business model sequence appropriate to current evidence.
slides.key_message: A validation service ladder lets AquaLoop create value before the hardware/system is fully proven.
slides.visual_direction: Three-step value ladder from assessment to pilot to system deployment.
slides.speaker_notes: Frame this as the practical route from idea to fundable/productizable proof.
production_notes: Pricing and margin model are not available.
slides.needs_input_reason: The offer ladder is a strategic recommendation that needs buyer and economics validation.

slides.title: Architecture should be selected property by property until engineering narrows the path
slides.purpose: Prevent premature commitment to one product architecture.
slides.key_message: Per-room, centralized, semi-centralized, and hybrid models have different operational risks, and none is proven yet.
slides.visual_direction: Architecture option matrix with benefits, risks, and validation status.
slides.speaker_notes: Keep the recommendation disciplined: compare before choosing.
slides.needs_input_reason: Engineering feasibility, cost, installation, and maintenance evidence are missing.

slides.title: The buyer map is multi-stakeholder until interviews prove ownership
slides.purpose: Clarify likely decision makers without inventing a buyer.
slides.key_message: Owners, operators, sustainability leaders, facilities teams, and MEP advisors may all matter, but budget ownership is unvalidated.

content_blocks.heading: Economic owner
body: Owner
Asset manager
Hospitality group leadership

content_blocks.heading: Operational owner
body: Property engineering
Facilities
Operations

content_blocks.heading: Influencers
body: Sustainability / ESG
MEP engineer
Developer or brand standards team

slides.visual_direction: Stakeholder map organized by economic, operational, and influence roles.
slides.speaker_notes: This is a hypothesis map for interviews, not a proven buying committee.
slides.needs_input_reason: No hotel buyer interviews or procurement evidence are available.

slides.title: Client-ready claims require five proof gates
slides.key_message: AquaLoop needs property, regulatory, engineering, buyer, and pilot proof before client-ready export.

content_blocks.heading: Proof gates
body: Property economics: water bills, demand, tariff, capex, opex.
Regulatory path: launch jurisdiction and AHJ/code review.
Engineering feasibility: architecture, treatment, installation, maintenance.
Buyer validation: budget owner, procurement, willingness to pay.
Pilot evidence: measured outcomes and operating reliability.

slides.speaker_notes: Turn uncertainty into a practical workplan.
production_notes: Add owners and dates once client chooses launch market and pilot path.
slides.needs_input_reason: Proof gates are defined, but no validation workstream has been completed yet.

slides.title: Visual direction should make water infrastructure concrete, not generic eco
slides.purpose: Guide design production for the deck.
slides.key_message: The design system should use hotel infrastructure diagrams, proof badges, and readiness gates rather than abstract blue-water sustainability visuals.

content_blocks.heading: Design principles
body: Show the building as the system.
Make uncertainty visible and useful.
Separate public evidence, assumptions, and blocked claims.

content_blocks.heading: Avoid
body: Generic water splashes.
Plug-and-play product visuals.
Luxury hotel photography that hides operational complexity.

slides.visual_direction: Mini visual system board with diagrams, badges, and restrained aqua/charcoal palette.
slides.speaker_notes: Use this to brief designers or preview production needs.
production_notes: Actual licensed imagery and diagrams are still needed.
slides.needs_input_reason: Visual assets are not produced or licensed yet.

slides.title: Package readiness is internal-draft-ready with client-ready blockers
slides.purpose: Close with the verifier decision and next inputs.
slides.key_message: The package is coherent and renderable, but client-ready export is blocked until core evidence gaps are resolved.

body.reason: Useful for strategy and validation planning
Missing buyer, property, regulatory, engineering, and legal proof

slides.visual_direction: Readiness gate table with clear pass/blocked labels.
slides.speaker_notes: End by naming what the team can do next rather than pretending the strategy is finished.
